coredata.dc:title: Biosensing platform for the detection of uric acid based on graphene quantum dots and G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Nan
Tan, Lu
Li, Yan
Xia, Tingting
Hu, Tianyu
Su, Xingguang
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a label-free biosensing platform for fluorescence detection of uric acid was designed on the peroxidase-mimicking activities of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme and the introduction of caffeic acid. Uric acid could be decomposed by uricase and then produced hydrogen peroxide and allantoin. We thus successfully achieved the indirect detection of uric acid by monitoring the concentration of hydrogen peroxide. The G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme could act as peroxidase and decompose the hydrogen peroxide into hydroxyl radicals at room temperature. Due to the strong oxidizing of hydroxyl radicals, caffeic acid was converted to corresponding quinone, thus leading to fluorescence quenching of GQDs. Under the optimized experimental conditions, the quenched fluorescence intensity was linearly relative to the concentration of uric acid, ranging from 2 μM to 300 μM with a detection limit of 500 nM. The applicability of proposed method was further proved with satisfactory results in human serum and urine samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Uric acid
G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme
Graphene quantum dots
Caffeic acid
Fluorescence detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of GQDs, caffeic acid and oxidized caffeic acid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The UV-vis absorption of (a) 15 μg mL−1 GQDs, (b) 500 μM caffeic acid and (c) 500 μM caffeic acid oxidized by uric acid, uricase and DNAzyme, and (d) the fluorescence emission spectra of 15 μg mL−1 GQDs.

Fluorescence intensity ratio I/I0 of the systems from a to h. I was the fluorescence intensity of (a) GQDs, (b) GQDs/caffeic acid, (c) GQDs/hemin, (d) GQDs/DNAzyme, (e) GQDs/uric acid, (f) GQDs/uric acid/uricase, (g) GQD/uricase/DNAzyme/caffeic acid and (h) GQDs/uric acid/uricase/DNAzyme/caffeic acid systems, respectively. I0 was the fluorescence intensity of GQDs. Concentrations: 1.5 μg mL−1 GQDs, 300 μM uric acid, 10 μg mL−1 uricase, 500 μM caffeic acid, 100 nM DNA, 1 μM hemin and 125 μM KCl.

Effect of hemin concentration (A) and incubation time (B) on the fluorescence intensity ratio I/I0. I was fluorescence intensity of the system containing 1.5 μg mL−1 GQDs, 150 μM uric acid, 10 μg mL−1 uricase, 500 μM caffeic acid and the DNAzyme (100 nM DNA, 1 μM hemin, 125 μM KCl). I0 was the fluorescence intensity of the detection system when the experiment started.

Effect of pH value (A) and temperature (B) on the fluorescence intensity ratio I/I0 of the system containing 1.5 μg mL−1 GQDs, 150 μM uric acid, 10 μg mL−1 uricase, 500 μM caffeic acid and the DNAzyme (100 nM DNA, 1 μM hemin, 125 μM KCl). I and I0 were the fluorescence intensity of the detection system in the presence and absence of uric acid, respectively.

Fluorescence emission spectra of the biosensing system with different concentrations of uric acid added (0, 2, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400 μM), the inset in Fig. 5 showed the correlativity between fluorescence intensity ratio I/I0 and the concentration of uric acid in the range of 2–300 μM. I and I0 were the fluorescence intensity of the detection system in the presence and absence of uric acid, respectively. Concentrations: 1.5 μg mL−1 GQDs, 10 μg mL−1 uricase, 500 μM caffeic acid, 100 nM DNA, 1 μM hemin and 125 μM KCl.

Fluorescence intensity ratio of the biosensing system with the addition of 300 μM uric acid or one of other foreign substances including 1 mg mL−1 HAS, BSA, Peps, Trys, ConA and 300 μM AA, GSH, Cys, Thr, Glu and NaCl. I and I0 were the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of uric acid or other substance, respectively. Concentrations: 1.5 μg mL−1 GQDs, 10 μg mL−1 uricase, 500 μM caffeic acid, 100 nM DNA, 1 μM hemin and 125 μM KCl.
Schematic illustration of the biosensing platform for uric acid determination.
Comparison of the linear range and detection limit among the designed method and other reported methods for uric acid detection.
Detection of uric acid in human serum and urine samples.

ce:note-para.ce:sup: The serum samples were 10 times dilution, and the urine samples were 50 times dilution.

head.ce:title: Biosensing platform for the detection of uric acid based on graphene quantum dots and G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nursing Department, Children's Hospital of Changchun City, Changchun, 130051, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a label-free biosensing platform for fluorescence detection of uric acid was designed on the peroxidase-mimicking activities of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme and the introduction of caffeic acid. Uric acid could be decomposed by uricase and then produced hydrogen peroxide and allantoin. We thus successfully achieved the indirect detection of uric acid by monitoring the concentration of hydrogen peroxide. The G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme could act as peroxidase and decompose the hydrogen peroxide into hydroxyl radicals at room temperature. Due to the strong oxidizing of hydroxyl radicals, caffeic acid was converted to corresponding quinone, thus leading to fluorescence quenching of GQDs. Under the optimized experimental conditions, the quenched fluorescence intensity was linearly relative to the concentration of uric acid, ranging from 2 μM to 300 μM with a detection limit of 500 nM. The applicability of proposed method was further proved with satisfactory results in human serum and urine samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Uric acid (2,6,8-trihydroxypurine, UA) is the final product derived from purine metabolism and mainly exists in serum and urine of the human body. As a kind of vital marker molecule, uric acid has been extensively used for physiological investigations and disease diagnosis [1]. In general, the normal ranges of human uric acid level are 130–460 μM in serum [2] and 1.49–4.46 mM in urine [3]. Studies have found that the uric acid levels in human serum and urine were closely related to some diseases such as hyperuricemia, gout and other secondary conditions [4]. Excessive uric acid could be caused by leukemia, renal impairment and cardiovascular disease [5,6], whereas lower uric acid levels might be linked to multiple sclerosis and pernicious anemia [7]. Hence, the level of uric acid was widely used as a measurable indicator in the clinical work. Under normal circumstances, the daily production and excretion of uric acid are basically keep dynamic balance, the factors which could influence the formation or the excretion of uric acid were all bring an anomaly of uric acid level. So distinguishing these two factors is very important for the clinical classification and medication guidance of certain diseases. In recent years, the amount of patients with high uric acid level has been shown an increasing and younger tendency. Such tendency is partly attributed to the developing economy, changing diet structure or growing food intake of high protein and purine. So far, certain progresses have been made for the determination of uric acid in the fields of bienzymatic colorimetry [8], radiochemical-HPLC [9], voltammetric detections [10,11], amperometric detections [12] and other electrochemical [13–15] or fluorescent techniques [1,16], but new systems with simple, efficient and low toxicity detection techniques were still urgently needed. Thus, designing an appropriate sensing system for uric acid quantitative determination has been attached with great importance to the human health.

In the last few years, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) which combined the attractive performance of graphene and QDs emerged and drew our attention [17–19]. The quantum confinement and edge effects of such zero-dimensional nanomaterials allowed them to possess high photoluminescence [20]. Studies also found that GQDs exhibited low toxicity, robust chemical inertness and good performance in photoluminescence, water solubility and biocompatibility [21–23]. For those reasons, GQDs has been widely used for cellular imaging, energy storage, disease diagnosis, drug delivery [24], electrochemical analysis [25] and fluorescence biosensing since its first synthesis [26,27]. Compared with conventional organic fluorescent dyes, GQDs has many distinctive optical properties as fluorescence probe, such as strong signal intensity, high quantum yield, tunable size-dependent photoluminescence and narrow emission peaks [28]. Recently, plenty efforts have been made to develop detection systems based on the fluorescence quenching of GQDs caused by electron transfer, such as the electron transfer occurred between GQDs and quinones, where GQDs acted as an electron donor [20,29].

DNAzyme is single-stranded DNA fragments with catalytic performance. As DNA molecule, DNAzyme possessed excellent thermal stability, resistance to hydrolysis and ability to copy, synthesise and modify [30,31]. When using DNAzyme as catalyst, the reaction conditions were not strictly limited by certain pH value or temperature like conventional protein enzymes. Previous studies revealed a strong connection between the catalytic property of DNAzyme and its structure which was readily influenced by the environmental conditions [32–34]. For instance, some specific guanine-rich nucleic acid sequences would fold themselves into four-stranded structure which was called G-quadruplex when inserted by chlorinated hemoglobin (hemin). Introduction of potassium ions can make the structure more stable [35]. The resulting G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme has been used as a biomimetic alternative of peroxidase because of the peroxidase-mimicking activities it displayed [36,37].

Compounds with the structure of quinones were known as electron acceptors which could induce fluorescence quenching of graphene quantum dots and caffeic acid (structure shown in Scheme 1 ) could be oxidized to that structure as a kind of plant-derived diphenols [38,39]. Herein, we designed a label-free biosensing platform for fluorescence determination of uric acid. The developed method was based on the peroxidase-mimicking activities of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme and the introduction of caffeic acid. The mechanism was demonstrated in Scheme 1. Based on the enzymatic reaction, uric acid was first incubated with uricase to produce H2O2 at 37 °C. Meanwhile, a selected G-rich DNA molecule folded into G-quadruplex structure in the system containing certain hemin and potassium ions. Then the formed DNAzyme could act as peroxidase and decompose H2O2 into hydroxyl radicals. Because of the strong oxidizing of hydroxyl radicals, the caffeic acid was converted to corresponding quinone, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of GQDs. In this way, a sensing system for fluorescence detection of uric acid was constructed. Compared with other analytical techniques, our fluorescence method was superior in rapid response and simple operation. The involved materials basically have low toxicity, which is meaningful to operator healthy and environmental protection. What's more, replacing protein enzyme by DNAzyme also provided us an easily controlled experimental condition.

All Chemicals used were at least of analytical reagent grade and were used without further purification. DNA oligonucleotides (base sequences shown in Scheme 1) were synthesized and purified by Sangon Biotech (Shanghai) Co. Ltd. Uric acid (UA), uricase (4.41U/mg), caffeic acid (CA) and hemin were obtained from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai) Co. Ltd. Ascorbic acid (AA), glutathione (GSH), potassium chloride (KCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), concentrated sulphuric acid (H2SO4, 98%), concentrated nitric acid (HNO3, 65%), concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl, 30%) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were obtained from Beijing Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co. Ltd. Tris(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane (Tris) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (China). Human serum albumin (HSA), bovine serum albumin (BSA), pepsin (1:3000), trypsin (1:250), and glucose (Glu) were obtained from Sino-American Biotechnology Co. Ltd. Concanavalin A (Con A), cysteine (Cys), threonine (Thr) were obtained from Shanghai Huishi Biochemical Reagents Co. Ltd. The deionized water we used in all experiments had a resistivity higher than 18 MΩ cm−1.

All fluorescence detections were carried out on a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu Co. Kyoto, Japan) equipped with a xenon lamp using right-angle geometry. Ultraviolet absorption spectra were recorded on a Varian GBC Cintra 10e UV-visible spectrophotometer. A 1 cm path-length quartz cuvette was used in the measurements above. All pH value measurements were performed with a PHS-3C pH meter (Tuopu Co., Hangzhou, China).

Graphene quantum dots were made from graphite oxide by means of a modified Hummer's method which we had carried out previously [40]. Simply, 0.3 g graphene oxide was first put in an Erlenmeyer flask. Then 60 mL concentrated H2SO4 and 20 mL concentrated HNO3 were added into the Erlenmeyer flask in turn. An ultrasonic processing was performed to the mixed solution for 2 h. After that, the mixture was kept under constant heating temperature of 80 °C with continuous stirring and the heating stopped 24 h later. After cooling down, the product was diluted with 600 mL deionized water and then its pH value was adjusted to 7.0 with 1 M NaOH solution. The resulting solution was further dialyzed through a dialysis membrane (retained molecular weight: 3500 Da) to remove excess salt.

The DNA samples were dissolved into 100 μM stock solution with deionized water and kept at 4 °C in the refrigerator. As for hemin, stock solution of 5 mM hemin was prepared in DMSO and stored at −20 °C. Before the experiment started, hemin stock solution was diluted 5 times with deionized water. After those preparations, 2 μL solutions of DNA and hemin were respectively added into 200 μL tris-HCl buffer solution (100 mM, pH 7.0) containing 1.25 mM KCl. Then the mixture was incubated for 30 min at room temperature to form the G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme with catalytic activity.

In this study, determination of uric acid was carried out in tris-HCl buffer solution (10 mM, pH 7.0) at room temperature. Uricase (10 μL, 2 mg mL−1) and different concentrations of uric acid solution (10 mM) were incubated at 37 °C for 30 min in the buffer solution to produce H2O2. We thus gain a set of samples containing gradient concentration of H2O2. Followed by the generated H2O2 solution was mixed with GQDs (200 μL, 15 μg mL−1), caffeic acid (50 μL, 20 mM, 10% DMSO) and G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme synthesized before. Then we diluted the resulting solution to 2 mL with deionized water in the specimen tube and shook up vigorously. The final concentrations of uric acid were ranging from 0 to 400 μM. Likewise, the final concentrations of GQDs, uricase, caffeic acid, DNA, hemin and KCl were 1.5 μg mL−1, 10 μg mL−1, 500 μM, 100 nM, 1 μM and 125 μM, respectively. The diluted mixture was allowed to take a thorough reaction for 60 min before fluorescence measuring. Fluorescence spectra were recorded in the 500–740 nm emission wavelength range with an excitation of 480 nm, the slit widths of emission and excitation were both set at 10 nm.

For real sample analysis, drug-free human serum and urine samples were collected from healthy volunteers at the Hospital of Jilin University. All experiments were carried out in compliance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines, and written informed consent for the samples obtained from human subjects. Before the experiment started, a pretreatment step was necessary to exclude the interferences caused by certain big protein molecules in the real samples. The serum samples were obtained through venipuncture. 15% trichloroacetic acid was firstly added into the serum samples (volume ratio, 3:1). After vigorously shaking for 15 min, the mixture was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. Then the obtained supernatant was adjusted to neutral. As for the urine samples, they were kept under centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 15 min. After those pretreatments, the centrifuged supernatant of serum and urine samples were then respectively diluted 10 times and 50 times with deionized water, and 200 μL of them was added in the 2 mL testing system mixing with GQDs, uricase, DNAzyme, caffeic acid. After that, different amounts of uric acid were added into the mixture to prepare a variety of spiked samples. The fluorescence measurements were performed before and after the standard addition of uric acid, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs, caffeic acid and oxidized caffeic acid
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 , Curve a and d were the UV-vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs, respectively. Curve a showed a typical UV-vis absorption peak of GQDs at around 300 nm, which was identical to the previous report [41]. Curve d had a fluorescence emission peak at 540 nm when excited at 480 nm. The shape of peak was narrow and sharp, which revealed that the as-prepared GQDs was mostly uniform in size. Curve b and c respectively exhibited the UV-vis absorption spectra of caffeic acid before and after the oxidation by uric acid, uricase and DNAzyme. By contrast, a distinctive absorption band appeared after oxidation. Previous work by Eyal et al. also found that the synergy of such G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme and H2O2 could catalyze the oxidation of a diphenol to a quinone [42]. So we thus infer that certain quinone was produced, and the oxidized caffeic acid was supposed to be caffeic acid-quinone. Such caffeic acid-quinone with the feature structure of quinone was known as an electron acceptor, which could lead to the quenching effect.

Then the separately influence derived from different component on fluorescence intensity of GQDs was studied. As shown in Fig. 2 , the fluorescence intensity remained almost the same in the systems from a to g. By comparing the fluorescence response in system g and h, we could find that the fluorescence intensity of GQDs was down to 70% of the original value after the addition of uric acid. Therefore, such prominent quenching performance implied that a reaction among uric acid, uricase, DNAzyme and caffeic acid occurred.

The fluorescence quenching efficiency of the established system close relied on some experimental conditions. In this work, we optimized the concentration of hemin, incubation time, pH value and reaction temperature of the biosensing system. Fig. 3 A showed the fluorescence responses with different amounts of hemin added ranged from 0 to 2.0 μM. The results indicated that the fluorescence intensity of GQDs had a continuous decrease from 0 to 1.0 μM and then went up slightly above 1.0 μM. According to Fig. 2 we knew that hemin did not have much impact on the system fluorescence performance. So we can infer that more G-quadruplexes/hemin DNAzymes were generated when the concentration of hemin rising, and the amounts of oxidized caffeic acid thus increased until reacted completely. Therefore, 1.0 μM hemin was chosen for further studies.

We sequentially optimized the incubation time. The fluorescence signal was recorded at 10-minute intervals from 0 to 80 min. As shown in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence intensity dropped sharply in the initial 40 min. Then the system needed about 20 min to reach equilibrium. Namely, the fluorescence intensity remained stable after reacting about 60 min. We thus selected 60 min as the incubation time in the following experiments.

According to the literature, the peroxidase-mimicking activity of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme was tightly tied to the value of pH and temperature. Fig. 4 illustrated the fluorescence intensity ratio I/I0 (I and I0 were the fluorescence intensity of the detection system in the presence and absence of uric acid, respectively) of this mimic enzyme catalysis procedure when the pH or temperature condition changed. As shown in Fig. 4A, the intensity ratio I/I0 decreased from pH 6.0 to 7.0 and then increased from pH 7.0 to 8.5. The degree of quenching reached maximum at the pH value of 7.0. Hence, pH 7.0 was chosen as the optimum pH value.

The optimization study of temperature was evaluated in Fig. 4B. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity ratio I/I0 decrease from 4 °C to 25 °C and then increased from 25 °C to 45 °C. This trend could be explained as follows: the activity of hemin changed over temperature owing to the existence of iron ions. As the temperature increased, hemin activities increased and then decreased. Thus, 25 °C was selected as the optimum temperature.

Under the optimized condition, the fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs/uric acid/uricase/DNAzyme/caffeic acid system with different concentrations of uric acid added were measured. As shown in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased along with the increasing concentration of uric acid from 0 to 400 μM. The inset of Fig. 5 showed that fluorescence intensity ratio I/I0 displayed a good linearity versus the concentration of uric acid in the range of 2–300 μM, while I and I0 were the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of uric acid, respectively. The linear regression equation was I/I0 = 0.98704–9.27054 × 10−4 CUA (μM) with a correlation coefficient R = 0.994. The detection limit of uric acid was 500 nM based on the 3σ rule. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for six replicate measurements of 50 μM uric acid in different days was 4.3%, which proved the stability and the reproducibility of the method.

Besides, the linear range and detection limit of some other methods reported earlier were summarized in Table 1 for comparison. It can be seen that our method offered an appropriate linear range and a higher detection sensitivity. Besides, our method also had unique advantages in terms of analysis time and system complexity. The procedure of replacing HRP by DNAzyme offered a simplified quantitative method, such procedure partly shortened the incubation time and simplified the components of detection system.

Aiming to assess the applicability of the proposed method for uric acid detection, the fluorescence response of solutions containing uric acid or foreign substance was examined under the same condition. The foreign substances included human serum albumin (HSA), bovine serum albumin (BSA), pepsin (Peps), trypsin (Trys), concanavalin A (ConA), cysteine (Cys), ascorbic acid (AA), glutathione (GSH), threonine (Thr), glucose (Glu) and NaCl. In the testing solution of 2 mL, the concentration of HSA, BSA, Peps, Trys and ConA was 1 mg mL−1. Besides those proteins, the concentration of other foreign substance was 300 μM, which was equaled to the concentration of uric acid. Blank sample was the system without uric acid or any other foreign substance added. As shown in Fig. 6 , the fluorescence intensity remained nearly unchanged when the foreign substances was added. And fluorescence quenching only occurred in the system containing uric acid. Thus the proposed system could achieve uric acid specific determination against the potentially co-existing substance mentioned above, which proved its applicability.

In order to prove the practical value in real sample analysis, the proposed platform was applied to uric acid determination in human serum and urine samples. The corresponding results obtained by fluorescence measurements before and after the standard addition method were listed in Table 2 . The average recoveries of uric acid in human serum and urine samples were 93.6%–105.3% and 92.5%–109.3%, respectively. Relative standard deviations (RSD) of the two groups of samples were both found within 5.0%. Therefore, results summarized in Table 2 further demonstrated the practical value of this method for uric acid detection in human serum and urine.

In summary, we constructed a biosensing system for fluorescence detection of uric acid based on the peroxidase-mimicking activities of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme and the introduction of caffeic acid. The caffeic acid could produced quinones under oxidization and thus led to fluorescence quenching of GQDs. In the quenching mechanism, the GQDs acted as an electron donor and the quinone acted as an electron acceptor. Good linear relationship and detection limit for uric acid were obtained under the optimized conditions. Satisfactory results in real sample detection further proved its applicability in human serum and urine. Compared with methods that use enzyme reaction, the proposed method offers a simplified quantitative approach. On the whole, this platform not only offers a simple, efficient and low toxicity approach for uric acid determination, but also widens the application of GQDs in biochemistry.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21075050 and No. 21275063), and the Science and Technology Development project of Jilin province, China (No. 20150204010GX).
